header.title: Safety and Shop Workflow
header.subtitle: Set up the bench before touching the engine.

graphics.title: Clean bench
graphics.caption: Keep grit away from crankcases and cylinders.

graphics.title: Eye protection
graphics.caption: Clips, springs, and solvent can launch unexpectedly.

graphics.title: Fuel control
graphics.caption: Work cold, ventilated, and away from ignition sources.

cheatSheets.summary: Most 2-stroke failures become expensive when dirt, old fuel, or guesswork gets introduced during diagnosis. This chapter gives you the order of operations.

steps.name: Make it safe
steps.goal: Cool engine, disconnect plug cap, drain risky fuel, and stabilize the machine.

steps.name: Record baseline
steps.goal: Note symptoms, fuel age, plug condition, compression, and visible leaks before cleaning everything.

steps.name: Test systems
steps.goal: Confirm fuel, spark, compression, and air sealing instead of replacing parts by habit.

steps.name: Repair one variable
steps.goal: Change one system at a time, then retest so the actual fix is known.

sections.title: Shop habits that prevent repeat failures

cards.title: Cleanliness
items: Cap open intake and crankcase ports
Use lint-free rags around bearings
Wash exterior dirt before teardown

cards.title: Documentation
items: Photograph linkages before disassembly
Bag fasteners by assembly
Write down jet sizes and screw turns

cards.title: Safety
items: Ventilate around premix and solvent
Use gloves for carb cleaner
Do not spark-test near fuel vapor

sections.title: Before the first pull
items: Fresh known-correct premix is in a clean container.
Air filter is present, seated, and not soaked with fuel.
Spark plug is correct type and properly tightened.
Throttle returns freely and kill switch is understood.
Cutting chain, prop, wheel, or attachment is secured before testing.

header.title: How a 2-Stroke Works
header.subtitle: Understand crankcase pumping, ports, and why small leaks matter.

graphics.title: Compression
graphics.caption: Piston rises and squeezes mixture in the cylinder.

graphics.title: Transfer
graphics.caption: Crankcase pressure pushes fresh charge through ports.

graphics.title: Power
graphics.caption: Combustion drives the piston and starts the next charge.

cheatSheets.summary: A 2-stroke makes power every crankshaft revolution, so fuel delivery, crankcase sealing, exhaust flow, and top-end condition are tightly connected.

cards.title: Fuel and oil
items: Premix or oil injection lubricates bearings and cylinder
Stale fuel gums carburetor passages
Too little oil can overheat and score parts

cards.title: Ignition
items: Spark must be strong under compression
Plug heat range and gap affect reliability
Weak grounds or kill switches can mimic carb issues

cards.title: Compression and sealing
items: Rings seal cylinder pressure
Crank seals support transfer flow
Reeds stop mixture from reversing through the intake

cards.title: Exhaust
items: Expansion chambers shape power delivery
Carbon can restrict small ports
Leaking flanges create noise and tuning errors

rows: Cylinder compresses mixture while crankcase draws fresh charge.
Low compression or leaking reeds can make starting difficult.
Ignition lights the compressed mixture before peak pressure is needed.
Weak spark often shows as wet plug and no fire.
Power stroke pressurizes crankcase and uncovers exhaust then transfer ports.
Blocked exhaust or air leak can flatten power.
Fresh mixture enters and scavenges exhaust from the cylinder.
Incorrect port, gasket, or piston orientation can disrupt flow.

sections.title: Rules of thumb
items: A 2-stroke needs fuel, spark, compression, and sealed crankcase pumping.
Do not tune around mechanical damage.
A lean condition can damage a 2-stroke quickly because fuel also carries oil and cooling.
Manufacturer specifications override generic pressure or screw-turn numbers.

header.title: No-Start Triage
header.subtitle: Separate fuel, spark, compression, and flooding in minutes.

graphics.title: Fuel
graphics.caption: Fresh premix reaches the carb and cylinder.

graphics.title: Spark
graphics.caption: A tester shows strong, repeatable ignition.

graphics.title: Compression
graphics.caption: Cylinder pressure is in serviceable range.

cheatSheets.summary: Good diagnosis uses a known sequence. Many small 2-strokes fail from fuel first, then ignition, then compression, but each system should be proven.

steps.name: Confirm fresh fuel
steps.goal: Use fresh premix, open fuel valve, and verify tank venting.

steps.name: Read the plug
steps.goal: Dry plug points toward no fuel; wet plug points toward flooding, weak spark, or low compression.

steps.name: Test spark safely
steps.goal: Use an inline spark tester or grounded plug away from fuel vapor.

steps.name: Try a controlled prime
steps.goal: If it briefly fires on a small prime, suspect fuel delivery or carburetor metering.

steps.name: Measure compression
steps.goal: If fuel and spark are present, stop guessing and gauge cylinder pressure.

rows: Check tank vent, fuel line, filter, carb inlet, and metering diaphragm.
Flooding, weak spark, or low compression
Install fresh plug, open throttle to clear, then spark-test.
Clean/rebuild carb and inspect diaphragms or float needle.
Inspect plug cap, coil ground, switch wire, and moisture.

sections.title: Stop and inspect if you see
items: Metal flakes on the plug or in exhaust oil.
Grinding, knocking, or locked crankshaft.
White, blistered plug after high-load running.
Fuel leaking from carb overflow or crankcase.
Compression far below the service manual range.

header.title: Spark Plug Reading
header.subtitle: Use plug condition as evidence, not folklore.

graphics.title: Tan or gray
graphics.caption: Usually normal operating condition.

graphics.title: Black deposits
graphics.caption: Often rich, fouled, cold plug, or weak ignition.

graphics.title: White or glazed
graphics.caption: Investigate heat, lean mixture, or air leaks.

cheatSheets.summary: NGK notes that light tan or gray usually indicates a normal condition, while wet or dry dark deposits and glazed overheating marks require follow-up diagnosis.

rows: Confirm gap, torque, and correct plug number.
Rich mixture, cold heat range, clogged filter, weak ignition, or extended idle.
Clean air filter, test spark, verify jetting and choke.
Flooding, excessive oil, weak spark, low compression, or rich metering.
Install fresh plug and check fuel/oil ratio, spark, and compression.
Stop high-load running; inspect air leaks, cooling, fuel flow, and timing.
Normal wear or extended service interval.
Replace plug and set gap to manual spec.

sections.title: How to get a useful reading

cards.title: Use a fresh plug
items: Old deposits can hide the current condition
Match heat range and reach to the manual
Gap before installing

cards.title: Match the test to the circuit
items: Idle reading reflects pilot/low circuit
Wide-open chop reflects main/high circuit
Cruise problems may sit between circuits

cards.title: Do not ignore symptoms
items: Pinging, heat, or power loss matters more than color alone
Wet plug plus low compression points mechanical
A perfect plug does not prove crank seals are good

sections.title: Plug service checklist
items: Use the exact manufacturer plug type or accepted equivalent.
Inspect porcelain for cracks and terminal for looseness.
Verify cap grips the terminal securely.
Apply torque from the plug maker or service manual.
Keep one known-good spare plug in the tool kit.

header.title: Fuel, Oil, and Storage
header.subtitle: Fresh premix is a repair tool.

graphics.title: Fresh fuel
graphics.caption: Gasoline can go stale fast in small engines.

graphics.title: Oil ratio
graphics.caption: Use the ratio specified for the engine and oil.

graphics.title: Sealed storage
graphics.caption: Cool, labeled containers reduce varnish and water.

cheatSheets.summary: Bad fuel causes hard starting, varnish, clogged passages, and lean damage. STIHL and other service references emphasize fresh fuel, accurate oil measurement, and manufacturer ratios.

cards.title: Stale fuel
items: Varnish smell
Hard starting after storage
Gummed carburetor metering passages

cards.title: Too much oil
items: Smoke and spooge
Carbon in exhaust
Fouled plug under low-load use

cards.title: Too little oil
items: Overheating risk
Piston/cylinder scoring
Bearing damage under load

cards.title: Water or ethanol issues
items: Cloudy fuel
Corrosion
Poor running after humid storage

sections.title: Mixing process
items: Check the manual for the required ratio and oil type.
Measure gasoline and oil with marked containers.
Mix in a separate approved fuel can, not the engine tank.
Shake gently, label the ratio and date, and use fresh stock first.
Discard old or suspect premix instead of tuning around it.

graphics.title: L screw
graphics.caption: Idle and low-throttle mixture.

graphics.title: H screw
graphics.caption: High-speed mixture and load safety.

graphics.title: Idle speed
graphics.caption: Throttle stop, not mixture.

cheatSheets.summary: Most adjustable 2-stroke carbs use low-speed, high-speed, and idle-speed controls. Make tiny changes, return to known baselines, and avoid lean high-speed settings.

steps.name: Verify fuel supply
steps.goal: Fuel flows from tank through filter and line without bubbles or blockage.

steps.name: Inspect air side
steps.goal: Filter is clean and carb boot is not cracked.

steps.name: Clean metering parts
steps.goal: Jets, screens, diaphragms, needle, and passages are clean and correctly assembled.

steps.name: Set baseline
steps.goal: Use factory screw turns or recorded starting points.

steps.name: Warm and tune
steps.goal: Adjust low circuit, idle speed, then high circuit under safe load.

rows: Bog on snap can be lean; burble and smoke can be rich.
Too lean may rev cleanly but run hot; safe tuning stays slightly rich per manual RPM.
Set so the engine idles without driving the attachment.
A stuck choke mimics rich jetting and fouls plugs.

sections.title: Carb rebuild warning signs
items: Starts on prime and dies repeatedly.
Diaphragms are stiff, curled, or cracked.
Primer bulb never fills or stays collapsed.
Needle tip is grooved or inlet screen is varnished.
Adjustment screws have little effect after cleaning.

header.title: Air Leaks and Reed Valves
header.subtitle: Lean symptoms often come from unmetered air.

graphics.title: Crank seals
graphics.caption: Leaks weaken crankcase pumping.

graphics.title: Reeds
graphics.caption: Petals must seal and not chip.

graphics.title: Pressure test
graphics.caption: Prove sealing before tuning.

cheatSheets.summary: A 2-stroke depends on crankcase pressure. Leaking seals, intake boots, gaskets, or reed valves can create lean running, hanging idle, and hard starting.

cards.title: Intake boot
items: Cracks open with vibration
Loose clamps bypass carb metering
Spray tests are only rough clues

cards.title: Crank seals
items: May cause hanging idle
Can pull transmission oil on some engines
Best confirmed with pressure/vacuum test

cards.title: Base gasket
items: Air leak at cylinder joint
Often appears after top-end work
Can make plug read lean

cards.title: Reed cage
items: Chipped petals reduce crankcase compression
Gaps allow mixture reversal
Wrong gasket can block flow

rows: Extra air keeps mixture lean after throttle closes.
Inspect boot, clamps, throttle slide, and seals.
Choke compensates for lean metering or leak.
Check fuel flow and pressure-test intake/crankcase.
Combustion heat rises with lean mixture.
Stop running hard until leak and jetting are verified.
Reed petal not sealing or intake pulse is unstable.
Remove reed cage and inspect petals and stops.

sections.title: Reed inspection
items: Petals sit flat against the cage with no visible daylight.
No chipped corners, frayed composite, or cracked steel.
Stops are not bent beyond manual spec.
Cage surface is clean and gasket faces are flat.
Fasteners use correct thread locking method where specified.

header.title: Compression Testing
header.subtitle: Measure before you rebuild or tune.

graphics.title: Gauge
graphics.caption: Use the right adapter and seal.

graphics.title: Wide open
graphics.caption: Open throttle while cranking.

graphics.title: Manual spec
graphics.caption: Generic psi ranges are only triage.

cheatSheets.summary: Small equipment references often cite roughly 90-110 psi as normal and below 70 psi as severe, while powersports engines may require higher values. Always compare with the service manual.

steps.name: Warm if possible
steps.goal: A warm, running engine gives a more realistic ring seal reading.

steps.name: Disable ignition/fuel safely
steps.goal: Avoid accidental start and keep spark away from fuel vapor.

steps.name: Install tester
steps.goal: Thread the adapter into the spark plug hole by hand and seal it.

steps.name: Open throttle
steps.goal: Hold throttle wide open so the cylinder can fill with air.

steps.name: Crank to peak
steps.goal: Pull or crank until the gauge stops rising, then record the value.

rows: Continue fuel, spark, exhaust, and leak diagnosis.
Rings may be worn or gauge/test method may vary.
Repeat warm with wide-open throttle and compare trend.
Worn rings, scored cylinder, damaged piston, or gasket leak.
Remove exhaust for visual piston/cylinder inspection.
Temporary oil seal points toward ring or cylinder wear.
Possible gasket, cracked piston, or test error.

sections.title: Avoid bad readings
items: Do not test with a closed throttle.
Use a gauge with a check valve near the adapter.
Charge battery on electric-start machines.
Repeat the test and record engine temperature.
Do not compare different gauges as exact truth.

header.title: Top-End Inspection and Rebuild
header.subtitle: Piston, rings, cylinder, clips, and gaskets.

graphics.title: Piston crown
graphics.caption: Arrow or mark faces correct direction.

graphics.title: Ring locator
graphics.caption: Ends must sit on pins and avoid ports.

graphics.title: Circlips
graphics.caption: Install fully seated and oriented correctly.

cheatSheets.summary: A top-end rebuild is precision work. The big failures come from dirt, wrong piston orientation, pinched rings, reused clips, and ignoring cylinder measurements.

cards.title: Cylinder
items: Look for vertical scoring
Measure bore and out-of-round
Confirm plating or sleeve service path

cards.title: Piston
items: Check skirt wear and crown wash
Match size and orientation marks
Replace after seizure damage

cards.title: Rings
items: Check end gap in bore
Confirm top side marking
Make sure ends meet locator pins

cards.title: Small end
items: Inspect wrist pin and bearing
Replace clips, do not reuse
Cover crankcase while installing clips

steps.name: Prepare parts
steps.goal: Wash cylinder, verify ring gaps, confirm piston direction, and oil bore.

steps.name: Protect crankcase
steps.goal: Stuff clean lint-free rag around rod opening before clip work.

steps.name: Install piston
steps.goal: Fit bearing, pin, and new clips fully seated in grooves.

steps.name: Compress rings
steps.goal: Align ring ends with locator pins and slide cylinder gently without force.

steps.name: Torque and recheck
steps.goal: Use pattern and torque specs, then pressure-test where required.

sections.title: Never skip
items: Use a service manual for torque, clearance, and ring gap.
Replace circlips and gaskets.
Point piston mark in the specified direction.
Oil cylinder and bearings before assembly.
Break in according to engine and ring manufacturer guidance.

header.title: Exhaust, Silencer, and Power Valve
header.subtitle: Backpressure, carbon, spooge, and moving valves.

graphics.title: Spooge
graphics.caption: Oil residue can point to rich or cold running.

graphics.title: Packing
graphics.caption: Silencer packing affects noise and flow.

graphics.title: Power valve
graphics.caption: Carbon can stick valve movement.

cheatSheets.summary: A restricted exhaust can make a good engine feel weak. Rich running and low-load use leave carbon that hides in ports, silencers, and power-valve assemblies.

rows: Rich jetting, too much oil, cold operation, or weak spark.
Confirm plug, air filter, carb settings, and operating temperature.
Plugged spark arrestor, collapsed packing, stuck power valve, or rich main.
Inspect and clean exhaust path before jet changes.
Damaged O-rings, springs, gasket, or bent pipe.
Replace seals and verify pipe seating.
Follow service manual inspection and adjustment procedure.

cards.title: Small equipment mufflers
items: Check spark arrestor screen
Remove carbon per manual
Avoid damaging catalyst-equipped mufflers

cards.title: Dirt bike chambers
items: Inspect dents and flange fit
Replace O-rings and springs
Repack silencer when oil-soaked

cards.title: Power valves
items: Mark linkage orientation
Clean carbon gently
Verify free movement before cover goes on

sections.title: After exhaust work
items: Start with known carb settings.
Check for leaks while cold and after heat cycle.
Listen for new rattles before riding or cutting under load.
Retorque accessible fasteners after first heat cycle if manual allows.
Recheck plug condition after sustained load.

header.title: Overheating and Seizure Clues
header.subtitle: Read heat damage before repeating it.

graphics.title: Heat
graphics.caption: Lean, timing, cooling, or load can raise temps.

graphics.title: Seizure
graphics.caption: Find the cause before installing parts.

graphics.title: Scoring pattern
graphics.caption: Damage location tells a story.

cheatSheets.summary: A seized 2-stroke is often a symptom, not the root cause. Air leaks, lean jetting, old fuel, wrong oil ratio, cooling blockage, or detonation must be corrected.

rows: High heat, lean mixture, insufficient oil, or restricted cooling.
Fuel flow, main jet, air leaks, cooling fins/radiator, exhaust blockage.
Warm-up practice, piston clearance, bore measurement.
Fuel octane, ignition timing, squish, air leaks, intake filtration.
Exhaust restriction, oil quality, jetting, service interval.

cards.title: Lean operation
items: Air leak
Clogged jet or fuel filter
Incorrect high-speed setting

cards.title: Lubrication
items: Wrong ratio
Bad oil
Oil injection failure

cards.title: Cooling
items: Blocked fins
Low coolant
Mud-packed radiator

cards.title: Assembly
items: Tight clearance
Wrong piston direction
Pinched ring

sections.title: Before the replacement top end
items: Pressure/vacuum test crankcase if the engine design allows.
Clean or replace carburetor parts that control high-speed fuel.
Verify cooling path and fan/shroud/radiator condition.
Check timing, plug heat range, and fuel quality.
Measure cylinder and piston clearances.

header.title: Maintenance and Long-Term Storage
header.subtitle: Keep small problems from becoming carb rebuilds.

graphics.title: Intervals
graphics.caption: Hours and seasons matter.

graphics.title: Air filter
graphics.caption: A dirty filter changes mixture and wear.

graphics.title: Storage
graphics.caption: Fuel decisions determine spring starting.

cheatSheets.summary: Fresh fuel, clean air, a good plug, and dry storage solve many 2-stroke complaints before diagnosis begins.

rows: A clogged filter richens mixture and raises wear risk.
Shows fouling, wear, and heat clues.
Every 30 days for unstabilized premix as a conservative rule.
Old fuel loses volatility and gums passages.
After rebuild and heavy vibration use.
Loose intake or exhaust parts create leaks.
At baseline and when power drops.

cards.title: Short storage
items: Use fresh stabilized fuel if recommended
Run long enough to circulate
Store cool and dry

cards.title: Long storage
items: Drain or run dry where manual recommends
Fog or oil cylinder if specified
Protect intake and exhaust from pests

cards.title: Return to service
items: Use fresh premix
Inspect lines for cracks
Check filter and plug before starting

sections.title: Field kit
items: Known-good spark plug and plug wrench.
Small fuel line and filter.
Correct screwdriver or limiter tool for carb adjustments where legal.
Compression tester adapter for your plug thread.
Zip bags, marker, and nitrile gloves.

graphics.title: Right tool
graphics.caption: Avoid rounded screws and cracked castings.

graphics.title: Measure
graphics.caption: Clearance and gap decide reliability.

graphics.title: Torque
graphics.caption: Use sequence and units from the manual.

cheatSheets.summary: A torque wrench, feeler gauges, micrometers, and a clean manual beat guesswork. Generic values are not a substitute for exact engine data.

rows: Feeler gauges with ring squared in bore.
Avoid cold seizure or piston slap.
Head, cylinder, intake, and exhaust fasteners.

cards.title: Carburetor screws
items: Use exact driver shape
Do not force limiter caps
Record turns before removal

cards.title: Soft aluminum
items: Start threads by hand
Use torque in stages
Repair stripped threads correctly

cards.title: Precision parts
items: Keep measuring tools clean
Measure at specified temperature
Compare to manual ranges

sections.title: When specs are missing
items: Find the model-specific service manual before internal engine work.
Use parts diagrams to confirm gasket order and fastener length.
Do not borrow torque specs from a different displacement.
Ask a qualified shop to measure plated cylinders if tools are unavailable.
Treat generic web values as triage only.

header.title: Research Sources and Field Notes
header.subtitle: What this guide is based on and how to use it safely.

graphics.title: Manual first
graphics.caption: Model specs always win.

graphics.title: Sources
graphics.caption: External references inform generic workflows.

graphics.title: Know limits
graphics.caption: Use a pro for unsafe or high-risk repairs.

cheatSheets.summary: This app organizes common 2-stroke repair knowledge into field workflows. It is not a replacement for model-specific service literature.

rows: Fuel, ignition, compression triage and small-equipment compression ranges.
No-start logic, wet/dry plug clues, fuel bypass testing, and 90 psi starting reference.
Normal, fouled, overheating, deposit, and wear interpretations.
Fresh fuel, 50:1 example with STIHL oil, alternative ratios, and shelf-life cautions.
H/L screw roles, rich/lean adjustment effects, and tuning sequence concepts.
Ring orientation, circlip placement, cylinder prep, and assembly cautions.

cards.title: Generic guidance
items: Use for diagnosis flow and learning
Expect numbers to vary by engine
Record your own baseline

cards.title: Manual-required work
items: Torque values
Clearances and ring gaps
Oil ratio and carb baseline

cards.title: Get help
items: Crank rebuilds
Severe seizure damage
High-performance jetting under load

sections.title: Content safety notes
items: Do not run an engine that shows lean overheating or seizure signs.
Do not defeat safety guards during testing.
Do not perform spark tests around fuel vapor.
Confirm legal carb adjustment rules for emissions-controlled equipment.
Use professional service for repairs beyond your tools and experience.
